title: Teaching
text:
My teaching interests are grounded in physiology, anatomy, disease ecology, wildlife biology, One Health, and applied conservation science. I am especially interested in helping students connect biological concepts with real-world animal health, environmental health, and conservation problems.



Human and comparative physiology
Human and animal anatomy
Wildlife disease ecology
Conservation physiology
One Health
Virology and host-pathogen interactions
Reproducible research using R and GitHub
Scientific communication






Experience supporting and teaching physiology content, including cardiovascular physiology, respiratory physiology, autonomic nervous system regulation, blood pressure, ECG interpretation, and integrated physiological responses.



Interest in teaching anatomy through comparative and functional perspectives, connecting structure to physiology, health, and disease.



I value mentoring undergraduate students through fieldwork, laboratory techniques, data analysis, scientific writing, and professional development. My goal is to help students see themselves as capable contributors to science, especially in Puerto Rican and island-based research contexts.




I believe science education should be rigorous, accessible, and connected to place. Students learn more deeply when they can connect biological concepts to the ecosystems, animals, communities, and public health challenges around them.
My teaching approach emphasizes:

Clear conceptual explanations
Applied examples from wildlife, veterinary, and human health
Student participation and discussion
Visual learning and data interpretation
Reproducible workflows
Community-centered science




This website will eventually host selected teaching resources, including:

Short physiology explanations
Laboratory guides
R and GitHub tutorials
One Health case studies
Wildlife disease ecology teaching modules

title: Publications & Presentations
text:
This page summarizes manuscripts, conference presentations, posters, protocols, and public-facing research outputs.




Working title: First epidemiological assessment of Toxoplasma gondii serological testing in Puerto Rico: an analysis of NBS human diagnostic records
This manuscript examines human Toxoplasma gondii diagnostic testing records from Puerto Rico to provide an initial epidemiological assessment of exposure patterns across demographic and regional variables.
Status: In preparation






Title: First Epidemiological Evidence of Human Toxoplasma gondii Exposure in Puerto Rico: a Retrospective Analysis of Diagnostic Records
Authors: Alan J. Figueroa Ruiz, Héctor G. Reyes Delgado, Karina S. Tapia Ortiz, and Alex J. Veglia
Event: Biology Symposium, University of Puerto Rico at Mayagüez
Format: Oral presentation




Poster presentations will be listed here as they are accepted and presented.

title: Outreach
text:
Community-based science is central to my work. I believe conservation becomes stronger when communities are not treated only as audiences, but as partners in understanding, caring for, and protecting local environments.


WildCiencia is a science communication and outreach initiative focused on making wildlife, conservation, and One Health science more accessible to the public.
The goal is to connect scientific research with communities, students, educators, and conservation partners through accessible language, visual communication, and culturally relevant storytelling.



My outreach work is shaped by the idea that communities can develop belonging in local spaces and are often the people who put conservation into action.
Areas of outreach interest include:

Wildlife conservation education
Manatee conservation
Environmental health
One Health
Pathogen movement across land and sea
Responsible pet ownership and cat-related environmental health
Student training and mentorship
Puerto Rican island science




The Manatee Project Puerto Rico integrates research, fieldwork, education, and community engagement to communicate how environmental health connects with the conservation of endangered marine mammals.
Future outreach materials may include:

Educational infographics
Field sampling explainers
Community talks
Short videos
School-based activities
Public-facing summaries of research findings




An important part of my work is helping undergraduate and high school students gain exposure to research. Training areas include:

Field sampling
Laboratory methods
Metadata organization
Scientific communication
Literature review
R and data analysis
Professional development




Future outreach events, photos, flyers, and public materials will be added here.

title: Alan J. Figueroa Ruiz
text:
Current focus
I am a master’s student in Biology at the University of Puerto Rico at Mayagüez, working at the intersection of wildlife disease ecology, conservation physiology, One Health, environmental health, and reproducible research.
My current research focuses on the land-sea transmission of Toxoplasma gondii in Puerto Rico and its potential conservation implications for the endangered Caribbean manatee. I am especially interested in how human-modified landscapes, stormwater runoff, feral and community cats, coastal ecosystems, and pathogen exposure connect terrestrial and marine health.

title: Alan J. Figueroa Ruiz
text:
Research themes


Wildlife disease ecology
Understanding how pathogens move through ecosystems and affect threatened wildlife populations.


One Health
Connecting human, animal, and environmental health through island-based conservation science.


Conservation physiology
Exploring how stressors, disease, and environmental change shape organismal health and survival.


Reproducible research
Using R, GitHub, bioinformatics, mapping, and open workflows to make research transparent and reusable.

text:

title: About
text:
About
I am a conservation agronomist and master’s student in Biology at the University of Puerto Rico at Mayagüez. My academic background combines Animal Science, Natural Resources Conservation and Development, and Environmental Sociology and Public Policy, giving me a multidisciplinary foundation in animal biology, conservation, environmental systems, and the social dimensions of natural resource management.
My research interests focus on wildlife health, conservation physiology, One Health, comparative anatomy, reproductive biology, and the effects of landscape change, anthropogenic activity, and climate change on threatened species. My current graduate research examines Toxoplasma gondii at the land–sea interface in Puerto Rico, with emphasis on pathogen movement from terrestrial environments into coastal ecosystems and its potential implications for the endangered Caribbean manatee. Through this work, I integrate disease ecology, physiology, wildlife conservation, environmental health, molecular diagnostics, bioinformatics, and community-based outreach. I am particularly interested in using veterinary medicine and biological sciences together to understand how disease, environmental stressors, reproductive processes, tissue-level changes, and physiological responses shape the health and survival of wildlife populations. This interest connects my current work in ecological disease and conservation with my broader goal of applying comparative anatomy, embryology, reproductive biology, and conservation physiology to the study and protection of endangered species.
I am deeply committed to community-based science because I believe communities have the power to reclaim, care for, and develop a sense of belonging within the spaces they inhabit, and that they are essential partners in putting conservation into action. I bring experience in conservation, animal science, research support, scientific writing, project coordination, environmental education, and community outreach. My long-term goal is to pursue combined DVM/PhD training and contribute to wildlife medicine, conservation research, reproductive and physiological approaches to species preservation, and the protection of endangered species worldwide.
More content coming soon.

title: News
text:
This page will document research updates, presentations, outreach events, publications, fieldwork, and professional milestones.




This website was created as a central space for my research, teaching, outreach, publications, projects, and reproducible science portfolio.



The human Toxoplasma gondii epidemiology project is being prepared for manuscript development. The project uses diagnostic records from Puerto Rico to assess exposure patterns and contribute to One Health research on the island.



The Manatee Project Puerto Rico continues to develop as a research program focused on environmental transmission pathways of Toxoplasma gondii and potential implications for the endangered Antillean manatee.
